Risks

Berkshire Hathaway is a huge, diversified conglomerate that was famously diversified, as many of the founders selling their businesses would stay on, continuing to manage their operations. This was one of the main selling points of Berkshire for acquisition targets.

But once those founders eventually retire, it is probably becoming increasingly difficult to manage operations effectively, and poor performance can start creeping in. Managing such a wide range of operations usually requires a sizeable central corporate operating function, which Berkshire does not have.

Conclusions

It may be difficult for Berkshire Hathaway stock to keep pace with the S&P 500 this year, given the index’s strong expected earnings growth. Broad market performance continues to be driven largely by the technology sector, with AI-related data center buildout boosting demand for electronics hardware. Berkshire remains underweight in technology and only held Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) briefly.

In the past, Berkshire’s strong organic growth may have offset its relative lack of tech exposure. More recently, however, performance across the group appears to have become more subdued, both in terms of earnings growth and capital deployment.

Greg Abel seems to have a challenging task ahead. On the positive side, Berkshire’s large cash reserves give the incoming CEO significant flexibility to pursue growth opportunities, including share buybacks.

data.title: Pilgrim's Pride: Cheap On Multiples, But The Cycle Is Rolling Over
data.description:
Pilgrim's Pride Corporation (PPC) is one of the cheapest packaged-food names on the Seeking Alpha Quant screen, with a 6.32x trailing P/E against a sector median of 16.56.

It is also a commodity chicken producer whose Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA margin collapsed 280 basis points y/y, whose forward EPS estimates have been cut 12% over six months, and whose one-year price performance is -39.58% against a sector decline of 6.59%.

The gap between those two facts is the entire investment question. Is this the deep-cyclical setup where the next leg is down but the valuation already prices it, or the one where cheap gets cheaper before it gets better?

The business in 60 seconds

PPC is the second-largest chicken producer in the US and the second-largest poultry producer in Europe. FY2025 revenue of $18.5B split 59.5% US, 29.1% Europe (UK's Moy Park and Pilgrim's Food Masters), and 11.5% Mexico.

PPC owns its feed supply, its hatchery operations, its grow-out farms, and its processing plants. Running all four steps in-house lets the company manage input costs and supply continuity more tightly than operators that buy protein at market rates along the way. What it does not do is create pricing power at the customer end. Fresh chicken is a commodity; buyers choose on price, and switching from one supplier to another carries no meaningful friction. JBS S.A. (JBS) owns more than 80% of the shares outstanding, so the minority holder owns the economic upside alongside an ever-present buyout-overhang risk. Net debt to EBITDA sits at 1.2x. The balance sheet is not the problem.

Three things matter

First, the valuation is real but not a free lunch. At 4.66x TTM EV/EBITDA and 5.71x on forward, PPC trades at a 57% TTM discount and a 40% forward discount to the packaged-food sector median. Even against its own five-year history, the forward EV/EBITDA of 5.71x sits 19% below the 7.04x five-year average. Chicken producers structurally trade below branded packaged food because chicken is a commodity with no pricing power, but PPC is cheap even within its own peer group. Cheap for a reason is not the same as cheap.

Second, the returns look moat-like at the cycle top and don't at the average. The trailing return on total capital of 15.32% sits 123% above the sector median. Return on common equity of 27.33% is more than double the sector's 10.65%. These look like the economics of a great business.

The ten-year average tells a different story: ROIC has averaged 10.48%, with collapses to near zero in 2020, 2021, and 2023. The 10-year average barely clears a credible cost of capital estimate for a commodity protein producer. Current returns don't annualize into the next decade.

Third, the cycle has already turned. Q4 2025 adjusted EBITDA of $415M was 21% below the prior-year quarter. Adjusted EBITDA margin of 9.2% compressed 280 bp y/y. Operating income fell 33%. EPS dropped from $0.99 to $0.37, a 63% decline.

US wholesale chicken prices have fallen roughly 20% since July 2025 as industry hatch rates caught up with demand.

Mexico's operating margin collapsed from 6.4% to 1.0% due to imported-protein pressure. In Q4 alone, the US GAAP operating margin dropped from 7.9% to 4.4%. This is not one soft quarter in a still-peaking cycle. This is the start of the down-leg, and forward EPS estimates are being cut in response.

The risk that breaks the case

The single biggest risk to even a neutral rating is that the cycle goes deeper and faster than the market is currently pricing. FY2021 saw adjusted EBITDA margin fall to 4.0%. FY2023 saw it fall to 5.7%. Both were triggered by the same dynamic now visible: hatch rates outrunning demand, feed-cost pass-through disappointing, and Mexico-US supply redirection pressuring local pricing.

A return to the FY2021 margin structure on the current $19B revenue base would drop adjusted EBITDA toward $750M, a 67% compression from FY2025. At a 6x EV/EBITDA multiple and the current $2.7B net debt load, that implies equity in the high teens. The single-digit P/E feels like a floor until you remember that in commodity businesses, it is the "E" that moves, not the "P/E."

Valuation anchored on mid-cycle, not peak

Normalizing EBITDA margin to the 6-year average of 8% applied to a $19B revenue base produces roughly $1.5B of mid-cycle EBITDA.

At a 6x multiple consistent with where PPC has traded mid-cycle, that is $9B of enterprise value. Subtracting $2.7B of net debt leaves around $6.3B of equity, or about $26 per share on 237.9M diluted shares. A cyclical-upside scenario at a 10% margin and a 7x multiple produces roughly $44. That is a wide distribution bracketing the current $33 price, with a central tendency that sits close to today's level.

Forward EPS consensus of $3.93 for FY2026 implies an 8.6x forward P/E, which looks cheap in isolation but reflects a -23.9% y/y earnings decline rather than a durable base.

SA Quant Rating breakdown

The Seeking Alpha Quant snapshot tells the same story as the fundamentals.

Valuation grades A (it's cheap on every multiple from P/E to EV/EBITDA to Price/Cash Flow).

Growth grades F because forward metrics have inverted: forward EBITDA growth of -4.7%, forward EPS growth of -9.38%, and a three-to-five-year EPS CAGR of -10.69% are all meaningfully below the sector.

Profitability holds a B based on above-sector return metrics (ROE 27.33%, ROTC 15.32%), but a gross margin of 12.75% against the sector's 35.79% is a reminder that commodity chicken carries structurally thin gross margins regardless of cycle position.

Momentum sits at D+ with the one-year price performance at -39.58% against a sector decline of 6.59%, and the six-month and three-month windows are similarly negative.

Revisions land at C- with forward EPS cuts of 4-12% across the one-month, three-month, and six-month windows.

That composite, cheap multiples paired with collapsing forward estimates and negative price momentum, is the classic value-trap signature rather than a deep-value entry point.

What to watch in the Q1 2026 print on 29 April

PPC reports first-quarter 2026 results after the market close on 29 April 2026.

Consensus expects revenue of $4.41B and normalized EPS of $0.65, against actual Q1 2025 results of $4.46B revenue and roughly $1.24 in GAAP diluted EPS. That is a 1.1% y/y revenue decline and a 48% y/y EPS decline already embedded in the Street number.

I think the print misses even that weakened bar.

Three reasons:

First, revision direction. Three analysts have cut their Q1 2026 EPS estimate in the past 90 days. Zero have raised. When 90-day revisions are unanimous on the downside entering a cyclical inflection, the realized print rarely surprises to the upside.

Second, US wholesale chicken pricing. EMI Whole Bird on Grid (the graph presented earlier in this article) shows pricing in early 2026 tracking below both the 2025 curve and the 5-year historical range. That chart covers the Q1 2026 reporting period directly. Price is the lever that drives margin, and the lever moved in the wrong direction for most of the quarter.

Third, the Q4 2025 exit rate. Adjusted EBITDA margin compressed to 9.2% y/y, the US segment adjusted operating margin dropped to 7.4%, and Mexico collapsed to a 1.0% operating margin. Q1 is usually sequentially stronger than Q4 on seasonality, but the $0.65 consensus EPS sits 76% above a depressed Q4 2025 print of $0.37 and requires a meaningful recovery in US and Mexico margins that the wholesale pricing data does not support. In a commodity rollover, the quarter immediately after the turn typically prints below both the prior-year comp and the analyst assumption that seasonality alone fixes the margin.

What to watch in the release and on the call

Management's commentary on US wholesale chicken pricing direction exiting March and into April, specifically any color on whether hatch rates are moderating or still running 3-4% above prior year. Any update on Mexico's import dynamics, the US tariff policy impact, and whether the 1.0% Q4 operating margin was a one-quarter trough or the new normal? Capital returns, specifically whether the company signals another special dividend at the Q2 declaration window; tone here is a proxy for management's own read on cycle durability.

A miss on both revenue and EPS with group adjusted EBITDA margin printing below 9% would confirm the cyclical trough is still ahead. A modest beat with margin stabilizing in the 9-10% band and constructive commentary on Mexico would not change the structural no-moat view but would shift the near-term setup closer to a tactical entry point. The bar that matters most is management's own forward language on wholesale chicken pricing, not the Q1 print itself.

My verdict

I rate PPC a hold. The valuation multiples are attractive in isolation but are actively being repriced downward as forward EPS estimates fall, Q4 2025 results confirm a cyclical rollover, and Mexico adds a fresh margin drag. Balance-sheet risk is contained at 1.2x net debt to EBITDA, and the JBS parent relationship reduces solvency risk further. The concern is the earnings line, which has a credible path to a lower trough before the next cycle up. I'd reconsider constructive positioning when either forward EPS estimates stop being cut for two consecutive months or adjusted EBITDA margin troughs below 7% and stabilizes. Until then, the quant composite and the fundamentals agree: cheap, but not a value.

data.title: Energy Transfer: The 7% Yield Leader Among America's Largest Midstream Operators
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Energy Transfer (ET) has the highest yield among the largest midstream operators at about 7%. This is due to its elevated leverage of around 4.25x; however, this leverage will provide higher returns as the US energy sector booms due to the Iran war.

Background

Energy Transfer is one of the largest and most diversified midstream operators in the United States. The company has operations in 44 states with around 140,000 miles of pipelines that span from crude oil to natural gas.

The company is very diversified, with NGL and refined products having the highest weight at 26%. In fact, it even has participation in other companies operating in other industries, like Sunoco (SUN), which is a fuel distribution company, or USA Compression Partners (USAC), a provider of natural gas compression services.

Moreover, the company is investing mostly in Natural Gas transportation and NGL, which I consider to be the segments with the best prospects given the expected increase in electricity demand. In fact, the company has already secured some contracts not only with power plants but even with Oracle itself to provide gas for three data centers.

Energy Transfer has planned growth CapEx of around $5.25 billion for 2026, which will allow the company to continue growing at least until 2030 as it targets returns in the mid tens.

Another characteristic of the company is that insiders own a substantial share of the company, which does not happen in other midstream operators. Insiders own around 10% of the company, and this ensures that despite having higher leverage than peers, management will try to secure the company’s future and maximize shareholders’ returns, as they are well aligned.

In that regard, the company expects to continue growing its distribution between 3% and 5% per year, and during the last few quarters, the company has been increasing it quarterly at $0.0025.

The current distribution implies a forward yield above 7%, the highest among its largest peers.

Financial Position and Valuation

Energy Transfer has a leveraged balance sheet with $1.2 billion in cash and $68 billion in debt at the end of Q4. However, despite leverage being around 4.25x debt/EBITDA, one of the highest ratios in the midstream sector, the company issued debt in January at attractive rates. For a comprehensive look at the maturity profile, I recommend reading Note 6 of its 10-K, where investors can see that the company has a staged maturity profile with maturities up to 2066.

This elevated leverage, compared to other midstream operators that typically have ratios of 3x to 3.5x, is a key characteristic of Energy Transfer. In fact, management’s target is in the 4.0-4.5x range. This allows the company to achieve higher returns, but the market perceives it as a higher risk. I would personally prefer the leverage to be slightly lower, but with high utilization the returns are better.

To partially mitigate this risk, the company has 90% of its business as fee-based; however, this is in line with other midstream operators.

This higher perceived risk by the market is directly translated into the yield the market demands.

Moreover, management provided strong 2026 guidance with an outlook to increase EBITDA to between $17.45 and $17.85 billion, implying around a 10% growth over 2025’s $16 billion EBITDA.

Given the Iran war and the surge in commodity prices, I expect the company to reach the higher end because it will benefit from higher prices and volumes, as I expect the US will maximize the export volumes of every energy commodity.

The Distributable Cash Flow is also strong; in 2025, it was $8.2 billion, and distributions amounted to $4.55 billion, providing a 1.8x coverage. For 2026 I expect the Distributable Cash Flow to be above $8.5 billion and the coverage ratio to increase slightly.

Regarding Q1 earnings, I don’t expect any surprises, as I don’t expect the Iran tailwinds to show until Q2. My estimation is for EPS around $0.35.

With all that in mind, I believe the stock could surge slightly above $20 per share; however, the main driver of the valuation continues to be its yield. I expect a $1.355 distribution in 2026, a fair value of $20 per share will imply a 6.8% yield. The highest among the largest midstream operators. This higher yield is justified by the higher leverage the company has.

For investors willing to receive higher yields with lower debt, I recommend taking a look into Western Midstream Partners (WES). It is a smaller midstream operator, but it offers a 9% yield with a leverage around 3x.

Risk

Energy Transfer’s main risk is the elevated debt. Any incident in its operations or external shock, such as COVID, could cause the company to halt or reduce the distribution to pay debt, directly impacting the stock price. This was demonstrated during COVID when the company had to slash the dividend when other midstream operators continued to pay their regular dividend. Additionally, the stock declined by more than 60%, while others declined by around 45%.

The elevated debt also makes the company exposed to credit risk. Energy Transfer needs to issue debt regularly; if credit conditions tighten, the company could be forced to sell assets at an inopportune time or issue additional units.

I would personally prefer that Energy Transfer reduce its leverage below 4x; however, it does not seem in the cards, and on the other hand, it allows shareholders to benefit from higher dividends.

Conclusion

Energy Transfer provides an attractive 7% yield, the highest among the larger midstream operators. This is justified as the company is the most levered, providing higher risk than its peers but also higher returns when the business is booming, as I expect it will over the next few quarters.

While it is not my base case, it wouldn’t surprise me if, in the second semester, management decides to increase its distribution by more than the usual $0.0025. That would be a great positive and could lead to a higher rerating of the stock.

data.title: Two Key Factors Driving The Economy, Neither Is Sustainable
data.description:
The markets continue a climb, an impressive 'wall of worry,' and are trading right at all-time highs. The Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed to almost all traffic for nearly two months now. This has caused energy and commodity prices to soar. The head of the IEA was on CNBC this week noting that the world is 'facing the biggest energy security threat in history' if this key chokepoint is not reopened soon.

Higher gasoline, diesel, fertilizer, and jet fuel costs are pushing inflation higher, and four-dollar-a-gallon gasoline has helped to push U.S. consumer sentiment to historical lows.

Things are worse in Europe and Asia, given these continents much greater dependence on key exports from the Middle East.

In addition, worries around private credit have soared in recent months. The U.S. housing market remains moribund, and delinquency rates on the approximately $5 trillion in commercial real estate debt continue to move higher. Job growth has been anemic over the past five quarters, with increasing concerns around AI's impact on the labor markets.

At the end of March, S&P Global was still projecting 2.2% GDP growth in the U.S. in 2026 and projects GDP growth will average 1.9% annually from 2027 to 2029. Interestingly, the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow Q1 GDP forecast has dropped from just over three percent at the start of the war in the Middle East to just 1.2%. And the conflict and its impacts were only felt in the last month of the quarter.

Second quarter GDP should be boosted marginally by increased tax refunds this year. That said, the U.S. economy seems to have very few drivers right now. And the longer this conflict drags on, the greater the impact on the U.S. and global economy.

In my view, the U.S. economy has two primary economic drivers that are left right now. The first is massive fiscal deficit spending. The other is the huge surge of tech spending around building out AI infrastructure. The problem with both of these factors is neither is sustainable over the long term or perhaps the medium term.

Key Economic Driver #1 - Massive Fiscal Deficit Spending

The fiscal situation in the U.S. was on an unsustainable path prior to the Covid pandemic. The U.S. had a fiscal deficit of $587 billion in FY2016, $666 billion in FY2017, $779 billion in FY2018, and $984 billion. The deficit blew out during 2020 and 2021 due mainly to Covid stimulus and other government programs. It has averaged nearly $2 trillion annually in recent years. This largesse has put enormous amounts of money into the U.S. economy, at the expense of the federal balance sheet.

The U.S. federal debt is rapidly approaching the $40 trillion threshold. And now the administration wants an over 40% increase in military spending in FY2027, which, if approved, would be the largest annual boost in defense spending since WWII.

Interest paid on the yawning U.S. federal debt has moved past the $1 trillion annual level. Interest is the second largest line item in the federal budget now, behind only Social Security. Although that could change in FY2027 (briefly) if the administration gets its $1.5 trillion request for defense.

Given the huge amount of governmental debt that continually needs to be financed, it is hard to see the ZIRP monetary policies that were in place for most of the 2008-2021 period appearing at any time again in my lifetime. Given the yawning deficits, it is hard to see the 10-year treasury yield getting below 4% for a sustainable period outside a major recession. Higher for longer interest rates are hardly the needed elixir for the moribund housing market or for the deteriorating credit ecosystem.

And while two percent annual GDP growth is expected on average currently from 2026-2029 in the U.S., it is certainly higher than that of Japan, Europe, and Canada. It is far less impressive considering the country's fiscal deficit has run at six to seven percent of GDP in recent years. The federal debt-to-GDP ratio is at its highest level since WWII. This is unsustainable.

Key Economic Driver #2 - Huge Increases In AI-Related Tech Spending

The second primary of the U.S. economy right now is the huge surge in tech spending around the buildout of AI infrastructure in recent years. This has mostly been spent on constructing massive AI data centers and the components that go into them across the nation. As you can see above, the four major hyperscalers have dramatically taken up their capex budgets in recent years. They will spend over $650 billion combined this year, after spending just over $350 billion in FY2025.

At first this burst of spending was funded from cash on the balance sheet and free cash flow at these hyperscalers. In recent quarters, more and more of this is being funded by debt issuance and Special Purpose Vehicles or SPVs. Free cash flow has plunged in recent quarters from these concerns. Key names in the AI ecosystem like Amazon (AMZN), Oracle (ORCL), and OpenAI (OPENAI) will see substantial negative free cash flow in FY2026, and for the latter two concerns, for many years into the future.

This enhanced spending and reduced cash flow mean much less money available for stock buybacks for key members of the Magnificent Seven, which has been a key driver for earnings in recent years. In addition, now the hyperscalers are reducing staff to free up cash for all this AI-related spending. On Thursday, Meta Platforms (META) confirmed it was laying off roughly 10% of its entire workforce, or roughly 8,000 positions. Meta also will not fill another 6,000 open roles.

Earlier this month, Oracle announced it was axing 30,000 positions, or nearly 20% of its global workforce, to free up capital for its cap ex spending. This week, Microsoft (MSFT) disclosed its first voluntary buyouts in its over half-century history that could reduce its workforce by up to 7,000 positions.

And the interesting thing to me is that despite dropping free cash flow and less money going to stock repurchases, earnings growth at the hyperscalers is still projected to grow at an impressive clip. Part of this is due to the vendor financing circle that makes what happened at the Internet Boom pale in comparison. This is a key source for future computing demand. OpenAI has made roughly $1.4 trillion in future commitments for this capacity, even though the company will not be profitable for many years.

It also should be mentioned that many planned AI data centers are falling behind schedule, and some are being cancelled. This is mainly due to the electrical grid having a hard time keeping pace with this huge new source of demand.

Finally, some of this earnings growth could end up being powered by overly aggressive depreciation schedules around GPUs.

Given these factors, it is hard to see how recent levels of annual tech spending growth will be able to be sustained in the near future. Add in the unsustainability of running current fiscal deficits, and the futures of these two main drivers of U.S. economic growth are cloudy at best. With all the other problems the U.S. economy faces that were listed in the opening narrative of this article, this likely means the U.S. economy will grow below current projections in the quarters and years ahead.

data.title: Why I Prefer Comstock Over CNX Resources
data.description:
CNX Resources (CNX) has long been a company that I prefer to avoid. The whole situation began with the take-under of CNX Midstream years ago. As I noted in one of my past articles, CNXM began trading at roughly $30 per share and was reacquired for between $8 and $9 per share. The midstream was in existence between 2015 and 2020. Shareholders of the midstream who held the whole time lost more than half of the original value. Since then, this management has not proven itself to be a better choice than some other alternatives. To me, management is the biggest asset or liability not on the balance sheet. Management can make an investment idea much safer or much riskier. The choices that have been made here have led me to keep this idea as a strong sell while purchasing Comstock Resources (CRK) as a strong buy idea.

Last Article

My last article reviewed the current situation here. Now higher gas prices may cause the whole industry to appreciate. But good management should do better under nearly any scenario. Therefore, I do expect an earnings boost throughout the industry thanks to storm Fern and the La Niña cold winter in general.

This was covered a bit more with my article on Kinder Morgan (KMI). If a midstream company is reporting more volumes handled due to cold weather, that implies that an upstream player like this one benefitted even more (financially) from that cold weather.

But I am not about to change my mind on a company like this just because the weather turned favorable for industry wide earnings. To me, the quality of the earnings and the company in general still make a difference. Therefore, I am attempting to sort through the whole situation to find the best industry picks because they will likely treat an investor the best rather than just picking "any" idea.

Debt

Chief, here is that the debt ratio is 2.0. That implies a fair amount of risk when many in the industry will tell you that the debt market prefers a debt ratio that is at most 1.6. 1.6 is the accepted upper limit when I have discussed this with various managements in the industry. But more importantly, lenders want to see free cash flow that can adequately service the principal payments. Here that may be an issue as well.

The management has long chosen to repurchase shares rather than make progress with the debt ratio. Even that debt progress had to deal with convertible debt. But the leverage strategy is a key financial risk that investors need to consider.

But the other thing is that the debt progress that was made was wiped out by the acquisition previously noted without really much more in earnings to make a debt ratio difference. The result is shown above with the debt just about where it was at the beginning of the slide (with only minimal progress).

But more importantly, there has been very little free cash flow diverted to debt repayment. Instead, the stock repayments have taken priority. That is generally seen as a financially risky way to operate in this industry.

Companies with leverage often have less maneuvering room than is the case with lower-debt companies. Debt has to be serviced and sometimes paid back no matter the industry conditions.

One would hope that the excess cash flow that many natural gas producers generated in the first quarter would be used to bring down that debt ratio. But here, the strategy could well have been to repurchase more shares "because they are cheap" knowing that higher natural gas prices would increase EBITDA to decrease the debt ratio.

The issue with such a strategy is that your principal can be at risk in the long term because this is a very volatile and low visibility industry. Oftentimes, debt turns out to not be the aid for larger long-term returns that many think it is in this industry.

In fact, the current leader for oil and gas in the newsletter is Headwater Exploration (CDDRF) with a 2000% return (so far) since management took over at $.40 (roughly) per share a few years back. That balance sheet has long been debt-free with a decent cash balance.

For this reason and several others, CNX Resources remains a strong sell.

Buy Comstock Resources

Before covid and a bit after covid, Jerry Jones invested roughly $1 billion in Comstock Resources. The investment virtually remade a company that was heading towards the corporate hereafter into a company with quite a future. The series of investments average around $7 per share and gave him control of the company.

The company now has a Western Haynesville discovery that could well remake the company's profitability. Since that discovery is now in the early stages of delineation (and derisking the acreage), it is no surprise that the stock is initially underperforming the industry.

But that could easily change as the company develops the new discovery acreage while learning the optimum way to do that development. Initial costs are on the high side (again, as might be expected). But management is working to get those costs down while also derisking and exploring the acreage potential.

Stock Price

The stock price initially nearly halved from the price Jerry Jones paid to roughly $4 per share, where it stayed for quite a while.

But once the promise of increasing exportability took hold from all the LNG projects underway, this company's stock price began to rally. The announcement of the Haynesville discovery sent the stock price to nearly $30 (combined with better industry conditions).

Now that the stock price has retreated to an attractive entry level. This management will be announcing the latest cost progress with the new discovery acreage at the next conference call.

In the long run, the company stands to benefit from the increasing exportability of North America as well as from a new discovery in the Western Haynesville. To me, this is a strong buy consideration.

Along with the discovery have been periodic cash raises either from Jerry Jones, the major shareholder, or by selling assets to fund the exploration and cost improvement initiatives. Sooner or later, that will not be needed anymore.

In this case, the strong natural gas prices of the first quarter could have provided an extra cash flow bonanza that would have enabled more money to be spent on the Western Haynesville discovery (and getting costs down). That would reduce the potential need for more cash.

Why Prefer Comstock Resources

Comstock Resources has a deep-pocketed major shareholder that has not hesitated to inject money into the company as needed, even after the original billion-dollar (roughly) investment. More importantly, there is a lot of anticipation surrounding the Western Haynesville not only from this company but also from others operating in this relatively new area (like Expand Energy (EXE)). This new basin is considered a major industry discovery. The costs need some work. But that is not unusual when the acreage is relatively new. As management learns about the acreage, the costs will come down still more than they already have.

On the other hand, when I first began coverage of CNX Resources along with CNX Midstream, the midstream was reacquired because the parent company needed the midstream cash flow. The reacquisition was, in fact, made with shares of the parent company (which led to share dilution).

Now, here we are with the debt at a similar level to several years ago (as shown above), and really there is not much free cash flow to handle the debt. That often limits the stock price appreciation potential. Debt ratios can be on the high side provided the market sees a way to pay that debt down. Right now, the progress shown above makes that pathway unclear.

The involvement of Jerry Jones has turned Comstock Resources into a growth story without the financial risk seen with CNX Resources. Furthermore, CNX Resources, despite the acquisition, does not appear to have a comparable growth story on the same scale as the Western Haynesville.

Summary

CNX Resources has very anemic free cash flow for the debt levels. Now that could change with the anticipated strong natural gas prices received in the first quarter. Additionally, the shareholders of CNX Midstream were not well treated when the take-under happened. Combine that with a fairly risky debt strategy of buying back stock rather than bringing the debt ratio down. To me the whole situation remains a strong sell.

I compare this to Comstock Resources, where Jerry Jones invested (initially) roughly $1 billion of his own money to remake the company. That company now has the Western Haynesville discovery, which it is rapidly moving to delineate and potentially develop. To me, this situation is a strong buy idea now that the stock has pulled back in the face of probably strong natural gas prices in the first quarter combined with the potential of the discovery.

Jerry Jones has already more than doubled the value of his initial investment. At one point, that was nearly a quadruple in the recent past. Yet he continues to hold most of the common stock in the company. I suspect he sees an even better future for the company (and more value for his investment) at current prices.

I would tend to hold Comstock Resources until either Jerry Jones sells the company or the story changes materially.

Risks

The anticipated long-term strengthening of natural gas prices could either abort or not happen entirely. Should commodity prices sustain a severe decline, it could materially impair the outlook for both companies.

Obviously, the Western Haynesville discovery could disappoint in part or on the whole in the future. Management could also fail to meet its cost goals that it set for the long term.

CNX Resources could change its strategy to both repay debt and considerably increase free cash flow throughout the business cycle. Such a strategy in the long-term could cause a reevaluation. Something like the take-under of CNX Midstream has to be completely "off the table" in the future. Investors will have to be served notice that they will be treated better in the event there is another cash flow issue.

data.title: Nordson Corporation: A Dividend King At Full Value
data.description:
Previous Coverage

I first covered Nordson Corporation (NDSN) late last year just prior to their Q4 2025 earnings. At that time, I rated the stock a HOLD as a result of mixed signals in their financials, recent earnings report, and a modest forward return potential, which was just under 9%. Since my initial coverage, the stock has been on a little bit of a roller coaster, rising more than 29% and peaking at more than $300 around mid-February, before retreating about 6.5% to settle where it is today. Despite the decline over the last two months, the stock has far outpaced the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) by about three times.

A few months ago, NDSN announced their Q1 2026 earnings report, so I thought now would be a good time to revisit the stock, as well as dive into that earnings call, and provide an updated valuation based on new free cash flow figures.

Q1 2026 Earnings & Looking Ahead

On Thursday, February 19th, the company announced its Q1 2026 earnings with in-line non-GAAP earnings per share of $2.37 on revenue of $669M, which surpassed the consensus by almost $17M and was a year-over-year increase of close to 9%. Additionally, GAAP earnings per share were $2.38, which exceeded expectations by $0.31.

Focusing on the Industrial Precision Solutions segment first, revenue increased 8.8%, rising from $300M in Q1 2025 to nearly $327M in the most recent quarter. This segment of the company accounted for almost half of all sales and was supported by organic growth of 3.2%, which was the result of increased demand in the Asia Pacific region, and overall solid results throughout the majority of divisions. Additionally, robust foreign exchange tailwinds also aided in these figures, positively impacting this segment's numbers by more than 5.5%.

Moving on to the Medical & Fluid Solutions segment, which saw an immaterial decrease in revenue, its portion of the company's sales dipped almost 300 bps to less than 29%, from 31.5%. This occurred, at least in part, because in September 2025 the company divested its medical contract manufacturing business, leading to a 4.3% headwind due to the sale. This deal occurred in order to allow the Medical business unit to focus on more high-margin and growth investments like proprietary medical components, i.e., cannulas or nitinol devices from the Atrion deal, which closed in Q3 of 2024. Conversely, organic growth for the quarter was 2.7% thanks to strong demand for specific fluid solutions, while FX rates increased results by 1.4%.

The final segment is Advanced Technology Solutions, which earned the biggest revenue increase at 23.1%, rising by $28M relative to Q1 2025. The bulk of this increase was organic and fueled by strong demand for powerful AI computing chips, which are supported by a complex manufacturing process related to NDSN tools. On top of that, the remaining 2.4% of growth occurred thanks to favorable foreign currency translation.

Management also provided guidance for Q2 2026, where they expect revenue to be in the range of $710M to $740M, equating to growth of more than 6% at the midpoint. Moreover, non-GAAP earnings per share are projected to grow by close to 16% at the midpoint of $2.80. The company also raised their outlook for the year, with the revenue range increasing from $2.83B-$2.95B to $2.86B-$2.98B. Similarly, the EPS range was also boosted from $10.80-$11.50 to $11.00-$11.60.

Initially following the earnings release, which can be read/listened to here, shares were rather mute, but as mentioned above, since then the stock has slipped lower by approximately 6%.

NDSN is expected to announce their next earnings call in about a month, where analysts are estimating Non-GAAP earnings per share of $2.85, compared to GAAP EPS of $2.52, and revenue is forecasted to be $733M. Additionally, of the seven analysts who cover NDSN, 6 of them have raised their EPS guidance in the last 90 days, while just 1 has lowered it.

In looking ahead to the next earnings call, investors should monitor the organic growth out of the three segments, which was a highlight in the most recent quarter, particularly in the ATS segment, which saw incredibly strong organic growth. Another area to assess is the company's margins, which topped 30% in Q1 and are likely to expand as a result of volume leverage, cost controls, and the company's "Ascend" program, which began in 2021 and is aimed at ensuring the company invests in high-growth, high-margin projects, driven by data and employees having an owner mindset. The final item investors should look for is the company's backlog growth, if any, as it increased by 4% in Q1, and management expects to see lingering effects in Q2 supported by continued momentum and a rise in order entries.

Bull & Bear Cases

Below are some bull cases for Nordson.

  • Advanced Technology Solutions has seen some ups and downs over the past six quarters, but when it's been good, it's been really good. Over the last year and a half, this segment has seen a decline in revenue twice, while the other four quarters have been outstanding, with revenue growing a minimum of 17%, and in two of those quarters, revenue surged more than 20%. Management stated in their recent earnings call that strong semiconductor demand in this segment has been a strong driver of the company's growing backlog, in addition to being one of the main reasons for the aforementioned increase in 2026 guidance.
  • Although Nordson has been around for some time, the company still continues to earn respectable organic growth in all three segments, as evidenced by the most recent quarter. Additionally, the company has a long track record of rewarding shareholders with more than 60 years of dividend increases and a disciplined capital allocation, which keeps the business in a healthy financial position. Essentially, NDSN has a proven track record of running a strong business, and there's no reason investors should be wary of the company at this time.

Conversely, here are a couple of bear cases for Nordson.

  • Nordson just posted a solid quarter, but the company does have elevated exposure to industrial markets, more specifically automotive production, where it sells adhesive control systems. This area of the market is particularly vulnerable to consumer spending and interest rates, which, as we all know, have been heightened for some time. If interest rates remain high, this could hinder future results, particularly in the Industrial Precision Solutions portion of the company.
  • Foreign exchange rates are a double-edged sword; they can obviously benefit you when favorable but also be a headwind. NDSN generates roughly ~60% of its revenue outside the United States, leaving it exposed to currency swings, ever-changing trade policies, and potential tariffs (should that become a big issue again). While this is nothing new to the company, it's still something they must contend with moving forward in order to remain successful.

NDSN Valuation & Return Potential

The final consideration is to decide if NDSN is trading for an appealing valuation. I explained my thought process and how to read the chart below in my first write-up, which can be read here.

The favorable market sentiment for Nordson over the last few months has pushed the stock higher, as well as the potential fair value for the business in my custom free cash flow valuation tool. Currently, my free cash flow model indicates Nordson is trading very close to its fair value, with a current price of ~$282, and a fair value closer to $277, equating to just a 2% premium at this time.

The long-term expected rate of return for NDSN has increased slightly from my last write-up, where it came in at 8.84%. As it sits now, this figure comes in at 9.60%, with the components of this estimate being a forward dividend yield of 1.16%, a return to fair value factor of -0.40%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 8.84%.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, Nordson has been on quite the run over the last five or so months, regardless of the recent price weakness. The company turned in a solid quarter with the Advanced Technology Solutions segment leading the way and showing incredible YoY growth, while the IPS segment posted decent numbers, and the MFS segment was flat. Management showed confidence in the business by raising its full-year 2026 outlook and pointing to meaningful growth in the next quarter. The stock appears to be trading for a reasonable price at this time, but I'd prefer to see a little bit of a pullback before expanding or initiating a position. For now, I'll rate the stock a HOLD but will keep a close eye on it around the next earnings call for any price weakness.

data.title: Columbia Banking: A West Coast Regional Player Growing Through Acquisition And Lending
data.description:
The Thesis: Reaffirm Prior Buy Rating

In the wake of bank earnings season, today I wanted to highlight one regional bank in particular that continually seems to prove my bullish case on it, and that is Columbia Banking System (COLB), based in the Pacific Northwest and parent of Columbia Bank.

On Apr. 23rd, it beat earnings estimates yet again, with 4 beats in a row now. In addition, it is up around +48% since my strong buy rating in Aug. 2023, and also up since my subsequent buy ratings in Sept. 2024, in Aug. 2025, and also in Jan. 2026. Some key factors that drove my confidence were a lower risk exposure, loan growth, and expansion via acquisition of Pacific Premier Bancorp.

My updated thesis today actually reaffirms my prior few buy ratings yet again. This time, from my 8-point weighted evaluation above, I thought that upside could come from a resilient macro environment for lending, company growth via recent acquisitions and new loans, an investment-grade balance sheet risk, proven dividend growth through various cycles, upside forecasts and bullish technicals.

Macroeconomic Factors

I was modestly bullish in this section after considering the combined factors below.

In trying to fit this bank into an adequate macroeconomic sector, considering both its income statement and recent Q1 earnings release, I would put it into regional banks in the western US that depend heavily on lending (interest income) and somewhat less on non-lending business.

Because some prior macro forecasts from late 2025 and early 2026 were projected before the current malaise in the Mideast and resulting oil shock, I turned to more recent forecasts on loan demand, and there happens to still be resilience, with the housing market in focus as an anchor again.

For instance, CNBC wrote this week that lower rates boosted borrowing demand:

Mortgage rates dropped for the third straight week, boosting demand from both homeowners and homebuyers. The spring housing market had been looking like a letdown, but there appears to now be new life.

I think that increased demand could be good news for smaller banks dependent heavily on lending.

However, because Columbia is more of a regional than a national player, what I think also matters are demographic trends in western US states. For example, we see from the Q1 investor deck (pg. 4) that COLB has a major exposure to states like Oregon, Washington (its home state), and California, among others.

I think that population growth trends in a state could also be a tailwind for banking services demand, so I considered data from stateline.org (published in March) showing that based on data from 2024/2025, states like Texas, Arizona, Florida, and North Carolina saw considerable growth; however, it seems that Washington State is also in the "blue" here as well with a growth trajectory, and that could be good news for this bank.

The exposure to California and Oregon, though, which do not appear to have had as much growth, could also be seen by some as a headwind.

Competitive Positioning vs. Peers

Next, I considered competitive positioning, and here I was neutral on this stock, as it has some interesting qualitative factors but also lagged behind peers in some quantitative metrics historically.

In picking 3 comparables this time, I wanted to pick truly regional banks and those in different parts of the US for a diverse sampling: Valley National Bancorp (VLY), Regions Financial (RF), and Bank OZK (OZK).

From comparables data, we can see that on a quantitative basis, COLB lagged behind 2 regional banking peers on RoCE, yet led the group with a 5-year revenue CAGR of +14.6%, telling me that it may perhaps be stronger as a top-line grower than an earnings grower.

Momentum data shows that its 10-year price return was negative, not even coming close to achieving alpha against a benchmark like the S&P 500, while Regions came close to it. This could point to a lack of investor momentum for COLB in the last decade. The last few years were also particularly challenging for this sector, given that Fed rate hikes could make short-term borrowing costs rise for banks.

On a qualitative basis, I think what could give COLB an edge is its growing and competitive position in the western US states, as I showed in the map earlier, positioning it as a credible regional player. Its Q1 investor deck (pg. 5), for instance, shows that COLB ranked 5th in market share in the northwest US region:

This is both great news and evidence that the "big" banking names are still a major brand presence in that region, and COLB has to compete with them both in lending and attracting deposits, and some of those big names include Wells Fargo (WFC), which is also based on the west coast.

Company Growth Factors

In this section, I considered company growth factors, and from that standpoint, I was highly bullish and gave this section full weight in my overall score since it has a fundamental impact on the business.

Notable about COLB is its acquisition of Pacific Premier Bancorp, which I think is growth through acquisition rather than organically. In fact, there was a story in MSN this week highlighting this merger and its value:

The Pacific Premier acquisition expanded the franchise across eight western states and sharpened the company’s positioning in Southern California.

As with any acquisition or merger, investors should consider the near-term risk of integration challenges.

As for organic growth, given that I said it is a regional bank very dependent on lending, organic loan growth can drive future interest income and therefore can be an upside driver. Notable from the Q1 deck (pg. 9) is that the company said its 2026 small business and retail campaign "has been successful in generating new SBA lending relationships."

In addition, the same deck (pg. 19) mentioned that "new origination volume in Q1 2026 was up 38% relative to Q1 2025, reflecting increased customer activity and the addition of bankers from Pacific Premier."

It is also worth mentioning that the bank's average loan and lease assets grew almost +27% YoY:

Besides the loan business, the non-interest income segment also saw YoY growth (comparing FY25Q1 vs. FY26Q1), as the investor deck (pg. 14) shows:

What I do like is the diversification beyond just interest income, and that could help drive further resilience, if they can continue to grow the fees-driven business more.

Complementing my bullish view is Seeking Alpha data showing expected FWD revenue growth of +14.1%, beating the sector median considerably.

Earnings Outlook

Moving on, here let's talk about the earnings outlook, and from the results in my worksheet above, I am taking a neutral view.

Because revenue and expense impacts can vary quarterly, to smooth things out I prefer considering margins and their trends. For instance, the operating margin comparison of this peer group puts COLB somewhere in the upper middle of this peer group, in a respectable position:

From peer comparables data, we also see the net profit margin is on the low end of this peer group.

I think two key factors that could negatively impact earnings for this type of business are squeezing interest margins and merger-related integration costs, since they recently had an acquisition. However, over the longer period, a merger could be favorable to costs. For instance, the Q1 earnings release (pg. 1) said, "Non-interest expense decreased by $18MM, due to lower merger expense and the realization of acquisition-related cost savings."

I expect the full synergy benefits of such mergers to be more apparent in the longer term.

For instance, the consensus is not so confident about EPS growth this year, but a considerable recovery is expected in FY27, it seems.

Balance Sheet Risk Profile

In this section, I briefly go over the balance sheet risk profile, and was modestly bullish about this section, which I'll explain below.

If you follow me on Seeking Alpha, you know I usually mention a stock's debt-to-equity, as I think it is a practical way to get a sense of leverage risk vs. peers. In this case, this entire peer group has a relatively low (below 1.0) D/E ratio, although COLB is the highest in the group.

From the perspective of credit rating agencies, the risk profile seems favorable, as the company site shows investment-grade BBB/BBB+ ratings from Fitch and S&P.

As for asset risk exposure, I think the key risk exposure in a regional bank could come from its loan book. From the Q1 investor deck (pg 19), we can see that their loan book is quite diversified across loan categories and regions, albeit all in the western part of the US:

Although the bank also holds US Treasuries, I give that some less attention than loans, because loan defaults or delinquencies can certainly impact a bank negatively. In one segment that often gets attention, office property, it seems the 30-90 day past due level is just 0.08% of office loans, a very inconsequential level (data source: investor deck, pg. 25).

As for credit charge-off trends, Q1 remained rather close to Q4, with earnings results saying, "Net charge-offs were 0.30% of average loans and leases (annualized), compared to 0.25% for the prior quarter."

As an analyst following trends/patterns, what would concern me would be the charge-off trend steadily climbing over several quarters and years.

Dividends Outlook

In terms of dividends, I was bullish in this section after considering yield, payout ratio, and proven growth.

Peer data shows that FWD dividend yield is nearly 5% for COLB, the highest in this peer group, and certainly could appeal to yield-focused dividend investors, which I am also.

The 5-year dividend growth of 5.4% also shows this bank was able to be resilient and grow the dividend through various market cycles and shocks of the last several years.

Although the data did not show a payout ratio for COLB, if you were to consider the income statement, it does show a dividend payout ratio towards the bottom, which was around 51% for the quarter ending in December. I think if it remains around that range or lower, it can add to the dividend safety of this stock, leaving enough cash buffer from earnings.

Valuation vs. Upside Potential

We've come to my discussion of valuation and price forecasts, and based on my worksheet above, which uses inputs from Seeking Alpha data as a source, I determined the actionable insight here is that this stock is valued relatively in line with the peer group average but also presents upside, so I am bullish here.

It seems the market expects this stock to perform in line with peers, which is realistic considering the profit margins I mentioned earlier are in a similar range in this peer group with no major outliers.

My forecast calls for a share price of $33.76 by Dec. 2027, implying a +16% upside, which is somewhat better than the average Wall St forecast of +8.8% (as of Friday). Both forecasts certainly justify a bullish view, on a stock reasonably valued.

Technical Charting Patterns

In this final section, we step away from fundamental analysis to consider some quick technical charting, with the chart below that tracks the share price over the last year vs. the 50-day and 200-day SMA (simple moving average).

From the pattern in the chart, it seems the stock recovered from its bearish dip, is trading above the 50-day SMA, which is now its support, and the long-term 200-day SMA continues to point upward. Overall, I thought this could be evidence for bullishness.

Key Risks to My Thesis

When thinking about what could challenge my continually bullish thesis, I think the biggest risk continues to be interest rate risk because not only does it put pressure on margins by raising the cost of borrowing but also it can impact the value of US Treasuries held on the books.

Systemic risks from the regional banking sector also can impact a bullish view, since another scenario like the failure of Silicon Valley Bank a few years ago could negatively impact investor confidence in this sector, sending down share prices if a major bank failure or FDIC takeover were to become public. There is the fear of contagion that is very real, even if another regional bank has strong fundamentals.

Conclusion

To conclude today's follow-up, I presented why I continue to think that COLB should be a buy, and the key strengths were driven by its business and organic growth along with factors like a favorable balance sheet risk.

My buy rating agrees with the overwhelming bullish consensus (quant rating, SA analysts, Wall Street) as of Friday.

The key takeaway I have for readers this time is to consider this stock both as a compelling dividend idea (yield, growth, safety) and also as a growth idea due to its forecasted upside and reasonable valuation right now. In fact, when constructing my own portfolio, I try to balance between dividend ideas and growth ideas, so definitely keep this one on your watch list for further research.

Research Methodology Used

The author rated this stock holistically across 8 weighted factors that include both fundamental and technical analysis, focusing on actionable insights geared to medium- and longer-term investors seeking both dividend and growth ideas through selective diversification.

data.title: China Automotive Systems: Still Worth Being Bullish On
data.description:
China Automotive Systems Inc (CAAS), a supplier of power steering components and systems for the automotive industry, continues to move sideways, a holding pattern the stock has been stuck in for over a year. Furthermore, growth prospects for CAAS has become more clouded with recent events in the Middle East, which could adversely affect growth, but which could also boost growth, depending on how events unfold. However, long CAAS is still the way to go, something the recent release of the last quarterly report for FY2025 on April 22 seems to concur with.

CAAS looks stuck

In my January 2026 article, I mentioned the failed attempt on the part of CAAS to overcome resistance, which had kept the stock contained for quite some time. This latest attempt several days earlier was not the first one, but the most recent one in a series of unsuccessful attempts by CAAS to attain a market cap beyond a certain level. However, while it was discouraging to see CAAS contained, there were also multiple reasons why CAAS had the potential to trade higher than it did at the time. Accordingly, I decided to rate CAAS a buy in the article.

The chart above of the past two years shows how CAAS failed to get past the $4.80-5.20 price region in January after reaching a 2026 high of $4.98, which was the most recent one in a series of failed attempts going all the way back to October 2024. Still, CAAS is up for the year with a YTD gain of 4.9% thanks to a move higher in the stock after the release of the Q4 FY2025 report on April 22, which erased earlier losses in the stock, losses that coincided with the outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East and the impact that had on stock markets.

The chart above of the past 12 months suggests CAAS is currently in a holding pattern. Note how the recent highs are trending lower, while the recent lows are trending higher. If all the low and highs are then connected with trendlines, then a descending upper trendline appears because of the lower highs and an ascending lower trendline because of the higher lows. The stock has been moving between these two extremes, something that could continue for some time as long as there is room to do so.

On the other hand, it’s worth noting how the trendlines are converging, which means CAAS will run out of room and it will eventually have to break through either the upper trendline or, alternatively, through the lower trendline. All this points to CAAS consolidating its position before embarking on what could become a substantial move higher, but also lower. For now, CAAS is in a holding pattern.

The Q4 FY2025 report had a lot for the bulls

The chart posted earlier shows how the stock jumped higher after the release of the Q4 FY2025 report. This is in part because while the stock price has not made much progress in quite some time, CAAS itself has continued to grow quite rapidly during this time. The latest report included, among other things, several new records, a sign CAAS is very much in a state of expansion.

Net sales increased by 21.4% YoY to $229.2M and operating income increased by 108.0% YoY to $18.1M in Q4 FY2025, which resulted in GAAP net income attributable to common shareholders of $18.4M, or $0.61 per share, double the net income of $9.1M or $0.30 per share in Q4 FY2024. The much improved bottom line benefited from gross margin reaching 23.1% in Q4 FY2025, up from 15.6% in Q4 FY2024.

On the other hand, it is worth mentioning that the latest quarterly results were likely an outlier. The increased profitability was the result of a change in product mix with higher contributions from higher-margin products, but also due to one-time benefits like tariff refunds. While the former can continue, the latter will not. Nonetheless, the strong end to the fiscal boosted the numbers for all of FY2025.

FY2025 net sales grew by 17.6% YoY to a record $765.7M. Operating income grew by 33.2% YoY to $53.6M and net income grew to a record $42.8M or $1.42 per share, an increase of 43.4% YoY compared to $0.99 a share in FY2024. Gross margin improved by 220 basis points from 16.8% in FY2024 to 19.0% in FY2025.

The income statement was not alone is posting big gains. The balance sheet also got much better with CAAS finishing FY2025 with cash, cash equivalents, pledged cash, short-term investments and long-term time deposits totaling $256.7M, up from $169.4M at the end of FY2024 and partially offset by short and long-term bank loans totaling $87M.

All in all, FY2025 was a good year for CAAS with progress on many fronts, including the top and the bottom line, even though it had to deal with various headwinds during the year. This includes the issue of tariffs, which are still around, although CAAS got some relief on this from with a favorable ruling by the U.S. Supreme Court.

What to make of the latest outlook?

However, if there was one blemish to what was for the most part a strong report from CAAS, it was guidance calling for revenue of $810M in FY2026, or an increase of 5.8% YoY. While this implies a new sales record since it will surpass the current one set just last year, it is also well below the 17.6% growth recorded in FY2025 and well below the CAGR of 13.08% sales grew by in the last three years or FY2022-2025.

While this number represents a preliminary and early view into FY2026 and it is possible for CAAS to revise the number during the year as more information becomes available, it still represents a soft outlook for the current fiscal. It also brings up the possibility CAAS may be affected by recent geopolitical events, particularly in the Middle East, which have resulted in much higher oil prices and other disruptions.

If inflation goes up as a result, a possibility that has gone up due to higher fuel prices, then that could affect the global economy. In general, when economic conditions deteriorate, then that tends to negatively affect consumer behavior. Consumers tends to postpone, if not cancel, big-ticket purchases if they sense leaner times are coming and this includes the purchase of an automobile.

Fewer car sales means fewer cars need to be produced, which in turn means fewer parts and components are needed, including those from CAAS. The current situation in the Middle East clouds the road ahead for CAAS because it could affect how the year plays out for CAAS, whether good or bad.

How CAAS could grow faster than expected

While growth prospects for the global economy has taken a hit from the rise in oil prices, there is a chance CAAS may surprise by growing faster than expected. This is because higher oil prices could increase demand for electrical vehicles, which is relevant to CAAS since China is the leading manufacturer of electrical vehicles.

CAAS does business with companies all over the world, but most sales are still to Chinese automakers like BYD. CAAS supplies power steering to both ICE and new energy vehicles, but if sales of the latter grow fast enough, it may be enough to offset the drop in demand the former is likely to suffer from higher oil prices.

It comes down to how the war in the Middle East evolves and how high oil prices get. If oil prices go up, but not so high that the world economy becomes so bad consumers stop spending, then consumers may still be willing to open their wallets just enough to keep enough cars, with a greater proportion of the electrical variant, moving. This would be best for CAAS with its strong ties to Chinese automakers, many of whom are among the leading sellers of electrical vehicles.

The risk for CAAS is that oil prices increase by so much and the economic hit to the world, including China, is so great that overall car sales contract by so much that it swamps whatever increased demand there is for electrical vehicles. If this happens, CAAS will probably be hard pressed to avoid underperforming in 2026 after a strong 2025. So how oil prices go is very important in terms of how the year plays out for CAAS. This in turn will depend on how the conflict in the Middle East develops.

Why CAAS is worth the risk

There is some uncertainty as to how 2026 will play out for CAAS because the conflict in the Middle East remains ongoing, but it can be argued that CAAS is still worth the risk because of where valuations are. CAAS, for instance, has a market cap of $134.9M with the stock last closing at $4.47 on April 24, which is less than the $142.0M in cash and cash equivalents CAAS has on the balance sheet.

Yes, CAAS has debt, but this is more than offset by all the other assets that can be used to pay off any debt out there. This includes $35.83M in short-term investments, $26.51M in long-term time deposits and $65.52M in long-term investments, which combined are more than what CAAS has in terms of debt.

In fact, based on the current balance sheet, CAAS deserves a much higher valuation with a book value of $13.30 a share, which means CAAS can be had at much less than its book value with a price-to-book of just 0.34x and the stock going for $4.47 a share. Valuations for CAAS are also low using other metrics.

For instance, CAAS is valued at just 0.18 times sales with TTM sales of $765.7M and a market cap of $134.9M. TTM GAAP EPS totaled $1.42, which accords CAAS a P/E ratio of just 3.15x with the stock last closing at $4.47. Not only that, CAAS could be undervalued at current valuations.

For instance, sales grew at a CAGR of 13.08% in FY2022-2025 as mentioned earlier, but earnings grew even faster by increasing from $0.69 in FY2022 to $1.42 in FY2025, which represents a CAGR of 27.2% during these three years. If CAAS manages to grow earnings anywhere close to a CAGR of 27.2% in the next three years or FY2025-2028, then according to the methodology favored by Peter Lynch, CAAS can be deemed undervalued relative to growth.

Even if earnings growth were to come at a much lower rate during FY2025-2028, CAAS would be undervalued as long as it achieves a CAGR of more than 3.15% during this time. Such a goal looks attainable for CAAS, considering the underlying fundamentals that are driving growth at CAAS remain in place.

Most of CAAS’ customers are automakers from China and these automakers have been gaining market share in recent years in most markets, in part because of a shift in consumer preference towards Chinese cars, especially when it comes to electrical vehicles. CAAS supplies these automakers, which allows for CAAS to grow on the back of this increase in demand. This has not changed, even with the recent headwinds in the world.

Investor takeaways

CAAS released what will be its last quarterly earnings report with the company shifting towards reporting twice a year starting in FY2026. The FY2025 report reaffirmed the notion that CAAS continues to grow and move forward, which stands in contrast to the lack of any significant movement in the stock price that has essentially been stuck below resistance for well over a year.

CAAS does face an uncertain outlook because of the impact higher oil prices could have on the global economy and demand in the market for automobiles, something that FY2026 guidance calling for just a 5.8% increase in sales did not put away. This is well below the growth CAAS achieved in recent years, including the recently completed FY2025.

In the worst case, oil prices could go so high, and, combined with fuel shortages, could do so much damage to the world economy that consumers start to pull back to such an extent that global car sales go down by so much that CAAS sees its top and bottom line shrink. However, this scenario is not pre-ordained or unavoidable.

It is quite possible for oil prices to stay high, like where they are now at around $100 a barrel, but not so high that the world economy cannot grow. If car sales do not drop by too much and sales of electrical vehicles make up a greater proportion, CAAS could still do well, possibly even surpassing expectations as defined by FY2026 guidance of sales growth in the mid single digits.

It really depends on how events in the Middle East unfold. While it is difficult to say with any certainty how the income statement will fare in 2026 due to the situation in the Middle East, CAAS still looks like a bargain based on the balance sheet. CAAS is worth slightly less than the cash and cash equivalents on the books in terms of market cap, which looks low considering CAAS is still growing and far away from posting any losses to warrant the very low multiples assigned to the stock.

I am therefore bullish on CAAS as things stand. The market for automobiles would have to really deteriorate to justify the low valuations assigned to a company like CAAS that actually set new records in the top and the bottom line in the latest report. This has yet to happen, nor is it destined to happen, and as long as it has not not, long CAAS is well worth the asking price.

data.title: Should Bitcoin Be In Your Retirement Account?
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has become a mainstream consideration for long-term investors. Here is what investors should know before adding crypto exposure to a retirement account.

For years, Bitcoin lived outside the traditional investment conversation. It was too volatile, too complex, and too unfamiliar to belong alongside stocks and bonds in a retirement portfolio.

That has changed. With the approval of spot Bitcoin ETFs in the United States, the maturation of the digital asset market, and growing institutional recognition of Bitcoin as a potential store-of-value asset and portfolio diversifier, a meaningful number of investors are now asking whether crypto belongs in their retirement account.

Since their January 2024 launch, U.S. spot Bitcoin ETFs have attracted approximately $96.5 billion in assets under management as of April 2026, reflecting significant institutional and retail adoption of regulated crypto exposure (Source: CMC Crypto News, April 15, 2026). The answer for any individual investor depends on their goals, risk tolerance, and how they structure the allocation. To understand the broader investment rationale for Bitcoin, including its potential role as a store of value and inflation hedge, start with VanEck’s full overview. To understand the broader investment rationale for Bitcoin, including its potential role as a store of value and inflation hedge, start with VanEck’s full overview.

Why Are Investors Putting Crypto in Retirement Accounts?

The retirement account conversation around crypto has accelerated as Bitcoin has grown into a significant and growing asset class held by institutional investors, sovereign wealth funds, and major corporations, among others. Its fixed supply, decentralized structure, and growing adoption as a potential store of value have prompted some investors to consider whether a small allocation may complement traditional assets in a long-term portfolio.

The macro environment has also brought more attention to digital assets. With U.S. federal deficits widening, global debt levels elevated, and inflation proving persistent, some investors view Bitcoin’s programmatically limited supply as a potential differentiator in an environment of ongoing fiscal expansion. These are considerations, not guarantees, and outcomes will vary based on individual circumstances and market conditions.

What Is the Case for a Small Crypto Allocation in Retirement?

Some investors consider a modest Bitcoin allocation in a retirement portfolio for several reasons:

  • Bitcoin has historically demonstrated periods of significant appreciation.*
  • Bitcoin has exhibited relatively low long-term correlation to traditional asset classes over extended periods, though correlations have varied meaningfully over shorter time frames and may continue to change.
  • Some investors view assets with fixed or limited supply as a potential hedge against purchasing power erosion over time. Bitcoin’s hard cap of 21 million coins makes it structurally distinct from fiat currencies.

How Does Holding Crypto in an IRA Reduce Tax Drag?

Cryptocurrency is treated as property by the IRS, meaning every trade, sale, or exchange may be a taxable event in a standard brokerage account. Practically, that means even swapping one crypto asset for another can trigger a tax event in a taxable account. Inside a tax-advantaged retirement account, these internal transactions generally do not trigger current tax liability, potentially reducing the drag of frequent trading or rebalancing. The specific tax treatment will depend on account type, individual circumstances, and applicable tax laws, which are subject to change.

How Does a Traditional IRA vs. Roth IRA Affect Crypto Tax Treatment?

Inside a traditional IRA, gains may grow tax-deferred until withdrawal. Inside a Roth IRA, qualified withdrawals may be tax-free under current tax law.**

Can You Add Crypto to Your 401(k)?

The regulatory landscape around crypto in 401(k) plans has shifted meaningfully over the past year. In May 2025, the Department of Labor rescinded its 2022 guidance that had urged fiduciaries to exercise “extreme care” before offering cryptocurrency in 401(k) plans, returning the agency to a neutral posture. In August 2025, Executive Order 14330 directed federal regulators to further reduce regulatory and litigation barriers to alternative assets in 401(k) plans. In March 2026, the DOL proposed a rule establishing a process-based safe harbor for fiduciaries evaluating alternative assets, including cryptocurrency, based on six factors: performance, fees, liquidity, valuation, benchmarking, and complexity. The public comment period on the proposed rule runs through June 1, 2026.

Even so, actual adoption at the plan-sponsor level remains in early stages. Most 401(k) menus do not yet include cryptocurrency options, and broad implementation will depend on the final rule, fiduciary considerations, and recordkeeper readiness. Investors whose plans do not offer crypto exposure may wish to explore alternatives such as IRAs or taxable brokerage accounts, where spot Bitcoin ETFs may be accessible.

What If Your Retirement Plan Does Not Include Crypto?

If your 401(k) does not offer crypto exposure, an IRA may be worth exploring as an alternative. Both traditional and Roth IRAs may be able to hold spot Bitcoin ETFs through standard brokerage platforms that support them, potentially providing access to Bitcoin within a tax-advantaged structure.

For investors who have already maxed out IRA contributions, a taxable brokerage account may still offer certain advantages, including access to spot Bitcoin ETFs and potential long-term capital gains treatment on positions held for more than one year under current U.S. tax law, which is subject to change.

What Are the Risks of Crypto in a Retirement Account?

Adding crypto to a retirement account involves meaningful risks that deserve careful consideration. Digital asset prices are highly volatile and can decline dramatically and quickly.

What Are the Key Risks of Bitcoin Compared to Traditional Retirement Assets?

Unlike stocks or bonds, Bitcoin does not generate earnings or cash flows, and its valuation is driven by factors including sentiment, adoption, and macroeconomic conditions. Regulatory uncertainty remains real, and the digital asset landscape continues to evolve. There is a significant risk of loss of your entire principal investment.

Given crypto’s volatility, even a modest allocation can meaningfully affect overall portfolio risk. Investors nearing or in retirement, where capital preservation is often a higher priority, should approach any crypto allocation with particular caution. Investors should consult a qualified financial advisor to determine whether digital assets are appropriate for their individual situation, goals, time horizon, and risk tolerance.

How to Access Crypto in a Retirement Account Using a Bitcoin ETF

The approval of spot Bitcoin ETFs in the United States has made it more accessible to gain Bitcoin exposure within a regulated investment vehicle. Investors may be able to hold Bitcoin exposure inside an IRA or brokerage account through a spot Bitcoin ETF without the complexity of managing wallets, private keys, or cryptocurrency exchanges.

Investors considering a potential allocation to digital assets within a retirement account should carefully review the fund’s prospectus, consider their individual circumstances, and consult a qualified financial or tax advisor before investing.

Important Disclosures

* Bitcoin has also experienced substantial drawdowns and past performance is not indicative of future results.

** Tax laws can change and individual circumstances vary. Investors should consult a qualified tax advisor before making any decisions based on tax considerations

Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC) is a decentralized digital currency without a central bank or single administrator. It can be sent from user to user on the peer-to-peer Bitcoin network without intermediaries.

Cryptocurrencies and digital assets are not suitable for all investors. Investments in digital assets and Web3 companies are highly speculative and involve a high degree of risk. These risks include, but are not limited to: the technology is new and many of its uses may be untested; intense competition; slow adoption rates and the potential for product obsolescence; volatility and limited liquidity, including but not limited to, inability to liquidate a position; loss or destruction of key(s) to access accounts or the blockchain; reliance on digital wallets; reliance on unregulated markets and exchanges; reliance on the internet; cybersecurity risks; and the lack of regulation and the potential for new laws and regulation that may be difficult to predict. Moreover, the extent to which Web3 companies or digital assets utilize blockchain technology may vary, and it is possible that even widespread adoption of blockchain technology may not result in a material increase in the value of such companies or digital assets.

Digital asset prices are highly volatile, and the value of digital assets, and Web3 companies, can rise or fall dramatically and quickly. If their value goes down, there’s no guarantee that it will rise again. As a result, there is a significant risk of loss of your entire principal investment.

Digital assets are not generally backed or supported by any government or central bank and are not covered by FDIC or SIPC insurance. Accounts at digital asset custodians and exchanges are not protected by SPIC and are not FDIC insured. Furthermore, markets and exchanges for digital assets are not regulated with the same controls or customer protections available in traditional equity, option, futures, or foreign exchange investing.

Digital assets include, but are not limited to, cryptocurrencies, tokens, NFTs, assets stored or created using blockchain technology, and other Web3 products.
